schema.name: adventure-knowledge-graph
schema.description: A stateful, directed knowledge graph for a cyber-incident crisis-management gamebook: Gold Command talks a ransomware attack on a district council through a 72-hour statutory clock. Same executable rule DSL as every adventure on this platform.

graph.title: Gold Command: The Mereford Incident
graph.subtitle: An interactive senior-management case study in cyber incident dependencies, competing duties and governance consequences — not a technical incident-response drill
design_notes: POSITIONING — be honest about what this is when introducing it: an interactive senior-management case study designed to expose how containment, staff comms, law enforcement, public comms, individual notification, the regulator and the insurer depend on and constrain each other, best used with a facilitator and a structured debrief. It is not a realistic simulation of Gold Command, not technical incident-response training, not formal data-protection training, and not a test of any real organisation's actual response plan. The mechanical judgement calls (isolate vs. verify, when to loop in support, how to sequence disclosure) are worth discussing; the specific numbers, timings and thresholds are a training abstraction, not a benchmark to hit.

There is no combat here. skill and luck carry the platform's standard test types instead: skill is technical/procedural judgement under pressure, tested at containment and at attribution, where the honest answer is ambiguous either time; luck is what the incident hands you regardless of judgement, sharpest at the recovery gamble, where refusing the ransom only pays off if the backups turn out as clean as the diagram claimed.

stamina is the one variable nobody puts on the incident dashboard: it only moves if Gold Command chooses to keep grinding through the night instead of resting, and running it to zero is the only way this story reaches ending_dead.

exposure is the sole bespoke stat, held to the platform's usual one-extra-stat pattern: a ratchet that only rises, standing in for how much of the incident becomes visible and reconstructable from outside the room.

cover_up_attempted and late_containment are two of the ways a Gold Command can lose the story even when the technical response is sound; team_trust and public_confidence were cut as stats that never earned their keep, leaving the flags to carry that weight instead. late_containment (set by m020, late network isolation) is a genuine consequence flag; it is not the same thing as any ICO-timing flag, and shouldn't be conflated with one — see the notes below on ico_notification_delayed / ico_notified_after_public_disclosure for why 'late' language around the ICO call specifically was removed.

law_enforcement_call and cyber_insurance_call model two real levers most ransomware training skips: reporting to Report Fraud (the national cyber crime and fraud service that replaced Action Fraud in December 2025) and requesting NCSC incident management support is a genuine, low-cost decision with a mechanical payoff (a stronger forensics test) rather than flavour text, and the insurer's refusal to cover a ransom payment is drawn from how UK cyber insurance policies actually treat sanctions-adjacent payment risk, not invented for drama. NCSC's incident management team, not the NCA, is the realistic source of 24/7 technical support for a council-scale incident — Priya C. is deliberately introduced here rather than at regulator_call, and regulator_call no longer repeats a separate NCSC contact, since a real council would already be in contact with the same team by the time it calls the ICO.

political_pressure's rumour_delivery is the closest a solo gamebook gets to the multi-stakeholder friction of a live tabletop exercise: two speakers, both right from where they're standing, neither of whom the player gets to cross-examine.

regulator_call and its flag (regulator_notified) refer to the ICO specifically, not to a bundled 'ICO/NCSC' action: the ICO notification is the actual legal duty the 72-hour clock enforces under UK GDPR, while an NCSC report is valuable, voluntary technical-advisory engagement with no statutory deadline of its own. The read_aloud narrates both calls happening together, as councils typically do in practice, but is explicit that only one of them is checked against a clock — don't reintroduce the conflation.

attacker_deadline_hours and comms_capital are load-bearing, not decorative: every route that represents genuine delay (verifying scope, waiting for forensic certainty, holding silence, the burnout grind, executive sign-off) spends attacker_deadline_hours, and spending enough of it locks the clean-recovery gamble (m080) behind a foreshadowed resource_at_least gate — dawdle for certainty and the option to refuse the ransom on your own terms can be gone by the time you reach it. comms_capital works the same way on the political axis, gating the integrity route through political_pressure (m070, requires comms_capital >= 3 of a starting 5): the reckless direct road (m011, cost 4) always forecloses it, the careful comms-led road (m043 cost 1, then m061 cost 1, total 2) always preserves it. Verify with real numbers before shipping a gate like this — the first version of this gate required more capital than any reachable path could retain, making the 'integrity' choice permanently unreachable.

m080 (the clean-recovery gamble) requires regulator_notified = true as well as enough hours remaining, and validation.ending_assertions enforces it: ending_recovery_strong's prose claims the regulator was notified in time, so no route may reach it without actually setting that flag. A route that grants the best ending without the mechanics to back it up is worse than no ending at all — it teaches the wrong lesson.

regulator_call's read_aloud_variants are keyed on exfil_confirmed as well as on the ICO-timing flags below: a player who ran the NCSC-supported logs test (m041b) before notifying has a confirmed fact, not a hedge, and the notification text has to say so. m041 itself stays a single, evidence-agnostic route ('on the evidence you have') precisely so the destination node's variant can carry the true framing — don't push evidence-state language back onto the route label, or the two will drift out of sync again the next time a route gets edited without checking the node it feeds.

m081 (paying the ransom) is a luck test, not a guaranteed purchase, on a document check of NCSC's actual current guidance (ncsc.gov.uk/guidance/organisations-considering-payment-in-ransomware-incidents, July 2026): NCSC and UK law enforcement do not encourage, endorse or condone payment, and are explicit that paying carries no guarantee of getting a working decryptor or any data back at all. A route that always pays off misrepresents that guidance as strongly as the earlier ungated ending misrepresented the ICO duty. Failure routes to ending_catastrophic — paid and still lost everything, the compounded worst case — with its own read_aloud_variant.

cyber_insurance_call and ending_ransom_paid both name the UK government's July 2025 consultation response committing to a targeted ban on ransomware payments by all public sector bodies (local government included), verified against gov.uk and the Home Office consultation outcome. As of this adventure's present day the ban is announced policy, not yet enacted law — the text is careful to say so — but it is real, current, and specifically on-point for a district council, and a training exercise that ignores it is behind the actual policy conversation its players will be having.

IMPORTANT — m060 and m070 do not, and should not, force a stop at regulator_call before reaching public_disclosure. That's deliberate, not an oversight: skipping the ICO notification has to be a real, choosable path or the m080 gate (regulator_notified required for the clean-recovery ending) isn't teaching anything, it's just decoration on a route that was never reachable anyway. public_disclosure's read_aloud correctly says so when it happens (a read_aloud_variant keyed on regulator_notified = false), rather than the earlier bug where it unconditionally claimed the regulator was informed regardless.

What was still missing, and is now fixed: skipping the ICO notification had a real cost but no way back — reaching public_disclosure with regulator_notified = false was a PERMANENT lock-out from ending_recovery_strong, with no route anywhere from public_disclosure or cyber_insurance_call back to regulator_call. That's not a real incident's shape: a Gold Command who realises mid-crisis 'we never told the ICO' picks up the phone, exactly as m050 already lets an earlier-stage player do. m076 (public_disclosure -> regulator_call, visible only while regulator_notified is still false) is that same recovery, later in the graph. It routes through regulator_call itself, then the existing m090 loop carries the player back to public_disclosure and on to the insurer as normal.

late_notification was a legal mistake, not just a naming one, and has been removed. UK GDPR's duty is notification 'without undue delay and, where feasible, no later than 72 hours'; the ICO's own guidance explicitly welcomes an incomplete initial report followed by phased updates. Nothing reachable in this graph gets anywhere near the 72-hour outer limit — even the slowest realistic path to any notification route lands well inside a single day — so treating any in-window notification as 'late' was factually wrong, and treating notification-after-public-statement as inherently late compounded it: the law does not require the ICO to be told before residents or the press, those are separate duties with no fixed ordering between them. Replaced with two honest, narrower flags: ico_notification_delayed (m050 only — you chose to wait for certainty; a legitimate judgement call worth a post-incident review asking whether reporting early on partial evidence would have served better, not a breach) and ico_notified_after_public_disclosure (m076 only — a sequencing fact, no penalty attached, because there isn't a legal penalty for it). Neither route costs exposure for the fact of delay or sequencing alone anymore; m042's own exposure cost (for the wait itself, not the eventual notification) is the only place that risk was ever legitimately priced.

affected_people_notified is new and deliberately distinct from both public_statement_made and regulator_notified. UK GDPR draws three separate lines: tell the ICO (regulator_notified, a duty to the regulator, checked against the 72-hour clock), tell the public generally (public_statement_made, reputational and practical, no statutory deadline), and tell the specific individuals at high risk directly (affected_people_notified, m077, triggered by risk severity — a stolen benefits-and-payroll export is exactly the kind of high-risk personal data that engages it — and discharged by neither of the other two). ending_recovery_strong's variant for exfil_confirmed = confirmed AND affected_people_notified = false gives this a real consequence: a second, distinct ICO finding, because a general press statement was never a substitute for telling the residents whose own data was taken.

This content revision was benchmarked against four documented, non-Gloucester real-world incidents rather than genre convention: Redcar & Cleveland BC (2020, ~£11.3m cost, 8.5-month recovery), Hackney LBC (2020, ICO reprimand issued in 2024 despite praised NCSC/NCA/Met engagement — reprimands for public bodies are the ICO's normal outcome, not a sign of a bad response), the HSE Ireland Conti attack (2021, PwC's Independent Post Incident Review — an eight-week undetected dwell time between initial compromise and ransomware deployment was the single most-cited finding), and Colonial Pipeline (2021, whose root cause — an unrotated legacy VPN credential — is the direct real-world model for this adventure's own hidden_truth). Three concrete changes came out of that comparison: forensics_engage's confirmed-exfiltration variant now reveals a six-week dwell time before the three-day-old exfiltration, so the story acknowledges that '06:10' was when the incident became visible, not when it began; ending_recovery_strong now costs millions and the better part of a year even in the best case, and closes with an ICO reprimand rather than 'no further action', matching Hackney's real outcome where good response and regulatory consequence for the underlying gap were not mutually exclusive; and staff_comms_dilemma exists because every real incident report in that set features a period where staff, press or the public learned things informally before anyone official told them, and none of this adventure's prior comms mechanics (political_pressure, comms_prep) touched the internal, staff-facing side of that problem or the specific dilemma of a compromised organisation not trusting its own email.

staff_comms_dilemma sits between contain_early and law_enforcement_call on the investigative branch (m010) only — the reckless direct-to-political_pressure branch (m011) skips it, consistent with that branch's established character of skipping process steps (it also skips law_enforcement_call and forensics entirely). staff_informed_safely and staff_comms_leaked are the two outcomes; the latter is read downstream by political_pressure's own read_aloud_variant, so an internal comms failure surfaces again as a concrete complaint from the Leader, not just a one-off consequence at the point of choice.

regulator_call's ICO case reference (RFA1122334) and named NCSC duty analyst (Priya C.) are illustrative, not drawn from a real case file — the point is procedural realism (a real incident generates a specific reference number and puts a named person on the other end of the NCSC line), not any claim to represent an actual ICO case.

MECHANICS — renamed hours_to_deadline to attacker_deadline_hours throughout: it was doing five jobs (attacker threat, press pressure, forensic pacing, restoration viability, insurer timing) under a name that implied it was also the ICO's clock. It never was — ICO compliance is represented purely by flags (regulator_notified, ico_notification_delayed, ico_notified_after_public_disclosure) precisely because no reachable path in this graph can breach the statutory 72 hours, so a second clock tracking the same number would carry no information. What the rename doesn't fix on its own: adding a genuinely separate ico_deadline_hours resource was considered and rejected, since it would tick in lockstep with the attacker's clock for the same reason and add a resource nothing can ever meaningfully read. If this graph is ever restructured so ICO timing can vary independently of the attacker's countdown, that's the moment to revisit this.

m080's gate moved from resource_at_least attacker_deadline_hours >= 40 to has_item backup_status_report (obtained via the new route m045, forensics_engage, 2 hours). Restoration viability is now about whether the recovery team actually verified the backups, not about how much of the attacker's arbitrary countdown happens to be left — matching how real recovery decisions get made. One consequence worth knowing: the reckless m011 branch (contain_early -> political_pressure directly) never visits forensics_engage, so it can never obtain backup_status_report, so m080 is permanently unavailable on that branch, on top of the comms_capital-gated loss of m070. That's a deliberate, compounding consequence of skipping process early, not a separate bug.

service_outage_hours is new: a resource incremented (not spent) by every route that represents time spent deliberating rather than delivering — verifying scope, waiting for forensic certainty, holding silence, executive sign-off. It's read via a resource_at_least variant on ending_recovery_strong, so a technically clean, well-sequenced response that nonetheless took its time gets an honest 'closer to a fortnight, not a week' instead of always claiming the fastest possible recovery.

DICE POLICY — m001 (immediate isolation) no longer rolls a skill test. Whether isolation succeeds is a matter of organisational capability established before the incident (segmentation, tested procedures, staff availability), not a coin flip Gold Command makes in the room; making a correct decision fail on a bad roll taught the wrong lesson. Isolating blind still costs a point of exposure, because the genuine uncertainty is what already left before the switch was thrown, not whether the switch worked. Remaining tests are all at genuinely exogenous uncertainty: m080 (whether the backups turn out clean given a verified report), m012 (whether the compromised-adjacent email channel holds), m044 (burnout), m081 (whether a ransom payment actually buys a working decryptor). Don't add a skill test to a route whose outcome should be determined by a prior decision or established capability — gate or modify the route instead, the way m041b's forensics modifier already rewards the law-enforcement decision without rolling for it.

ITEMS — nine artefacts exist (ransom_note, soc_alert_extract, supplier_vpn_log, backup_status_report, insurance_policy_wording, draft_public_statement, affected_data_schedule, leaders_office_email, reporters_questions), all granted, none orphaned. Only two are mechanically gated on (backup_status_report at m080, affected_data_schedule at m077) — that's a scope decision, not an oversight: the other seven exist to make key moments tangible ('here is the specific document/log/email this decision produced or required') rather than abstract flag-flipping, matching the brief's 'evidence, not just narrated choices'. Gating every item would turn artefact collection into busywork; gate the ones where possessing the evidence is genuinely the difference between a decision being informed or assumed, and let the rest do their job as texture. If a future edit wants more artefact-gated decisions, prefer the m077/m080 pattern (has_item as the actual legality condition) over adding items nothing ever checks.

HUB (v0.2.0) — gold_command_hub converts the front section (staff comms, law enforcement/NCSC reporting) from a fixed linear queue into two genuinely order-independent workstreams: h001 and h002 are both available from the hub immediately, each disappears once resolved (via its own flag, either outcome), and h003 (into forensics_engage) is never gated on either, since m040/m041b already read law_enforcement_notified live and gain nothing from an artificial hub-side gate. This is a deliberately bounded rewrite, not the full parallel-workstream vision (technical/service-continuity/legal/comms/law-enforcement/insurer/governance as simultaneous hub spokes) — that fuller version was scoped and rejected for this pass because it would mean rebuilding the entire back half of the graph (political_pressure's three-way branch, the comms_capital gate on m070, the evidence gates on m080/m077, the ICO-recovery route m076) that took several previous revisions to get right, for comparatively little additional teaching value: that half of the graph already isn't a fixed queue. Converting the one part that genuinely was — the fixed staff-comms-then-law-enforcement-then-forensics chain — captures the real lesson (Gold Command allocates attention across workstreams, doesn't personally complete a checklist in a fixed order) at a fraction of the risk to mechanics that took real iteration to verify. If this graph is restructured further, extend the hub pattern (spoke disappears once resolved, no artificial ordering gate between independent workstreams) rather than inventing a new one.
graph.narrative_premise: 06:10 on a Monday. The SOC has flagged mass file-encryption across Mereford District Council's shared drives. A ransom note names a 72-hour window before stolen data is published. You are Gold Command. Every choice you make in the next three days will be reconstructed later — by the ICO, by an FOI request, or by a journalist — whether or not it feels like it matters now.
graph.core_mystery: How far in did they get, and can you prove it before the clock forces you to guess in public?
graph.dramatic_question: Is the honest version of events worth more than the managed one?
graph.hidden_truth: The intrusion came through a third-party print supplier's VPN credentials, reused from a breach two years ago that was never rotated.

opening_context.player_facing_introduction: Your phone lights up at 06:10 with the kind of message that ends a normal week: SOC has flagged mass file-encryption across the council's shared drives, and a ransom note is already sitting in three inboxes, naming a 72-hour deadline before stolen data goes public. You are Gold Command. Nobody has briefed the Leader yet. Nobody has told the press office. Right now it is just you, a laptop, and a clock that started running before you woke up.

condition_dsl.combination: All conditions on a route must pass unless the route uses an explicit any block.

test_dsl.notes: 2d6 against stat plus modifier. skill is tested at containment and at attribution; luck is tested at the backup-integrity gamble and, optionally, at the burnout risk. There are no combat encounters.

regions.name: Mereford District Council, Incident Room
regions.theme: a 72-hour clock, a laptop, and every decision reconstructed later

narrative.surface_read: SOC dashboards, a borrowed meeting room, a ransom note nobody has stopped rereading.
narrative.deeper_truth: The technical incident is survivable; what actually ends careers is what gets said, or not said, while it's still live.
narrative.dramatic_function: Keep every choice legible as both an operational decision and a future line in an ICO report.
sensory_palette: a phone that will not stop
the ransom note's flat, rehearsed prose

items.name: The Ransom Note
items.description: Flat, rehearsed corporate prose, sitting in three inboxes before anyone in the building has finished their coffee: pay within seventy-two hours or the stolen data goes public. It reads like it was written for a dozen other councils first.

narrative_semantics.origin: Delivered automatically alongside the encryption, timestamped seconds after the first files locked.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: The clock the attacker wants Gold Command watching, as opposed to the clocks that actually govern the response.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: Its payment instructions are the first hint — to anyone who reads them closely — that this group is plausibly sanctions-adjacent.

items.name: SOC Alert Extract
items.description: The raw detection log: mass file-encryption events across the shared drives, timestamped, unreviewed, arriving faster than anyone can read them.

narrative_semantics.origin: Generated automatically by the council's monitoring tooling at first detection.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: What Gold Command actually knows at 06:10, as distinct from what gets assumed or narrated in the following hours.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: Read carefully, it shows detection — not compromise. The compromise happened earlier; this is only the moment it became visible.

items.name: Supplier VPN Access Log
items.description: Login records for the print supplier's VPN account, stretching back weeks further than anyone in the room expected to look.

narrative_semantics.origin: Pulled during the forensic logs review, once someone finally asks how the attacker actually got in.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: The dwell-time proof: six weeks of quiet access before the three-day-old exfiltration.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: A credential reused from a breach two years ago, never rotated, sitting in a system nobody was watching closely enough.

items.name: Backup Status Report
items.description: A verified account of what's actually recoverable: which images are clean, which are reachable from the compromised network, and which were never as segmented as the architecture diagram claimed.

narrative_semantics.origin: Compiled by the technical team once someone stops assuming the backups are fine and actually checks.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: The difference between hoping a restore will work and knowing whether it can.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: Segmentation was real but incomplete — which is why refusing the ransom is a genuine gamble, not a guaranteed win, even with this report in hand.

items.name: Cyber Insurance Policy Wording
items.description: The actual clauses, not the summary: what's covered, what's excluded, and the sanctions carve-out nobody reads until the day it matters.

narrative_semantics.origin: Retrieved from the policy file the moment the insurer's incident line is called.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: A decision that someone else has already priced, whether or not Gold Command likes the price.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: Restoration and forensic costs are covered. A payment to a sanctions-adjacent actor is not, and voids the policy outright.

items.name: Draft Public Statement
items.description: Comms and legal's working draft — what's confirmed, what's still assumed, and the gap between the two that the final wording has to resolve one way or the other.

narrative_semantics.origin: Drafted jointly by comms and legal once the decision is made to loop them in early.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: The moment private uncertainty has to become public language.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: What gets cut from it, and by whom, says more about the organisation than what stays in.

items.name: Affected Data Schedule
items.description: The specific list: whose benefits records, whose payroll details, exactly what left the network and who it belongs to.

narrative_semantics.origin: Produced by forensics alongside confirmation that exfiltration actually happened.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: The difference between 'a breach occurred' and knowing, specifically, whose lives it touches.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: A general press statement was never going to substitute for this list reaching the people named on it.

items.name: Email from the Leader's Office
items.description: Three lines, sent before anyone from the Leader's office has actually spoken to Gold Command: services back, disclosure minimised, come and explain in person.

narrative_semantics.origin: Sent the moment word of the breach reaches the Leader's office through channels other than Gold Command's own briefing.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: Political pressure arriving in writing, before it arrives in the room.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: The instruction is deniable by design — nothing in it could be read back as a direction to conceal anything, and everything in it points that way.

items.name: A Reporter's Questions
items.description: A voicemail and a follow-up email, both patient, both asking the same thing in slightly different words: why have council tax payments failed, and why won't anyone say.

narrative_semantics.origin: Left by a local reporter during the wait for forensic certainty, when the silence has gone on long enough to be noticed from outside the building.
narrative_semantics.symbolic_role: The outside world's patience, running out on a schedule nobody inside the room controls.
narrative_semantics.world_revelation: The questions aren't hostile. They're the questions any competent local reporter would ask, which is precisely what makes them hard to dismiss.

nodes.title: 06:10, The Call
nodes.summary: SOC reports mass encryption across shared drives; a ransom note demands payment within 72 hours or stolen data goes public.
nodes.read_aloud: Your phone will not stop lighting up. SOC has flagged mass file-encryption across the council's shared drives, spreading fast enough that nobody has a clean count yet. A ransom note is already sitting in three inboxes, its prose flat and rehearsed: pay within 72 hours or the stolen data goes public. Every minute of hesitation lets the encryption spread further; every minute you don't spend, you can't get back. Whatever you decide in the next sixty seconds, someone will ask you to justify it in writing before the week is out.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: 06:10 again, in memory if nowhere else: the phone, the flat ransom prose, the clock that started before you were awake.

narrative.present_tension: Every minute of hesitation lets the spread grow, but acting on incomplete information can cause its own damage.
narrative.hidden_truth: The intrusion came through a third-party print supplier's VPN credentials, reused from a breach two years ago that was never rotated.
sensory_details: a phone screen that won't go dark
the ransom note's flat, corporate prose
cold coffee, untouched
narrative.emotional_undertone: The particular loneliness of being the first person who has to decide anything.

nodes.title: Cut and Contained
nodes.summary: Isolation lands fast. Spread is limited, but council tax, benefits payments and the print supplier link all go dark with it.
nodes.read_aloud: The order goes out and the network drops behind it: council tax, benefits payments and the print supplier link all go dark in the same stroke as the encryption's spread. It is the right call and an expensive one, and you will not know for hours which of those two facts residents will remember first.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The network stays down, quiet in the particular way of a system that has been switched off rather than merely broken.

narrative.present_tension: Containment bought you time; it did not buy you certainty about what already left the building.
sensory_details: dashboards going grey one by one
a silence where the helpdesk queue used to be
narrative.emotional_undertone: Relief with a bill attached.

nodes.title: The Blast Radius Grows
nodes.summary: While you verify, encryption reaches the finance and benefits payment systems. Exposure rises.
nodes.read_aloud: Verification takes the time it takes, and the encryption does not wait politely for you to finish it. By the time the scope is clear, it has reached the finance and benefits payment systems. The number of people whose money is now delayed just became a number you will have to say out loud, later, to someone who wants to know why.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The blast radius as confirmed: finance, benefits, a spreadsheet of consequences that keeps growing while you read it.

narrative.present_tension: Verification was the responsible instinct; it was also expensive, and the expense is not yours alone to pay.
sensory_details: a scope document that keeps needing new rows
the benefits team's phone, ringing and ringing
narrative.emotional_undertone: Being right about caution and wrong about the cost of it, in the same hour.

nodes.title: Gold, Silver, Bronze
nodes.summary: Formal command structure costs you twenty minutes but buys coordination you'll need on day two.
nodes.read_aloud: You spend twenty minutes nobody wants to spend standing up a formal Gold, Silver and Bronze structure before touching the network switch. It feels like theatre while the fires are still small. It will feel like the only sane decision you made once day two arrives and six different departments need to be told six different things at once, in the right order, by someone whose authority isn't in question.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The command structure holds, minutes spent long ago on a decision that is still paying for itself.

narrative.present_tension: Structure now is time you don't have; structure later is coordination you won't have.
sensory_details: a whiteboard filling with names and roles
the specific relief of someone finally being told what to do
narrative.emotional_undertone: Discipline that looks like delay until it doesn't.

nodes.title: Gold Command
nodes.summary: Not a task list. A set of workstreams that all need addressing, none of which has to wait for the others to finish first.
nodes.read_aloud: This is the job, properly understood: not personally working every problem in sequence, but deciding what needs addressing, in what order, by whom, while everything else keeps moving. Right now there are at least two things that can't wait — what staff are told, and whether this gets reported — and neither one depends on the other being finished first. The investigation can start whenever you're ready for it to.

read_aloud_variants.text: Both of the things that couldn't wait have been addressed, for better or worse. What's left now is the investigation itself — and unlike the last two decisions, this one only has one road, not three.
Law enforcement is sorted, one way or the other. Staff still don't know anything official, and the helpdesk queue is not going to stay patient forever.
Staff have been told something, one way or the other. The question of who else needs telling — Report Fraud, NCSC — is still open, and it's not getting easier to answer by waiting.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: Back at the centre of it. Whatever's still open is still open; nothing here resolves itself while you're elsewhere.

narrative.present_tension: The temptation is to work these one at a time, in the order they occurred to you. Nothing about the incident requires that order.
sensory_details: a whiteboard with three columns and none of them empty
two phones and a radio, all live at once
narrative.emotional_undertone: Competence that looks like delegation, not busyness.

nodes.title: Who Tells the Building?
nodes.summary: Eight hundred staff are about to try to log in to systems that no longer work, and the fastest way to warn them is the one channel nobody can currently vouch for.
nodes.read_aloud: The helpdesk queue is already forty deep with people asking why they can't get into the case management system, and it is going to be four hundred deep by lunchtime. Somebody needs to tell eight hundred staff what's happening before rumour does it for you — and the obvious tool for that, the all-staff email, is the one thing nobody in this room is prepared to vouch for. The intrusion came in through a VPN account with valid credentials. Nobody yet knows how far the attacker got, or whether a mailbox is still being read by someone who isn't you.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The helpdesk queue is still climbing. The question of how to tell people anything at all is still open.

narrative.present_tension: The fastest channel is the one you trust least, and the channel you trust most is the slowest one you have.
narrative.hidden_truth: The attacker's access to the network died with the initial containment; nobody currently on the network can read outgoing mail. But nobody in the room knows that for certain yet, and 'probably fine' is not something you can put your name to in writing.
sensory_details: a helpdesk queue counter climbing in the corner of a screen
a draft email open in a mail client nobody's sure is safe to send from
a WhatsApp group someone started without being asked to
narrative.emotional_undertone: Not trusting the one tool built for exactly this moment.

nodes.title: Whose Investigation Is This?
nodes.summary: Before forensics goes deep, someone asks the question nobody has yet: has anyone reported this to Report Fraud and asked NCSC's incident management team for support?
nodes.read_aloud: Someone in the room finally asks the question that's been sitting there since 06:10: has anyone reported this to Report Fraud, the national cyber crime and fraud service, and asked NCSC's incident management team for support? Reporting now, before the logs are even fully read, hands the crime report to people who do this for a living and puts a formal evidence trail under everything forensics finds from this point on. It also means outsiders are in the room while you're still working out what actually happened. Waiting costs nothing on the surface — until the day someone asks why they were the last to know.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The question is still open: report it now, or once there's something more definite to hand them.

narrative.present_tension: Reporting early buys formal support and a clean evidentiary chain; reporting late buys you room to work without an audience, at the cost of that same chain.
sensory_details: reportfraud.police.uk open in a browser tab, not yet submitted
a duty log with a gap where 'reported' should be timestamped
narrative.emotional_undertone: A call that costs nothing to make and everything to have skipped.

nodes.title: What Did They Take?
nodes.summary: Forensics works the logs. The exfiltration claim in the ransom note may be real, may be bluff — the evidence is ambiguous under time pressure.
nodes.read_aloud: Forensics gets to work on the logs, and the ransom note's claim of stolen data sits there unresolved: it might be real, it might be bluff dressed up to look real, and the evidence available inside the deadline is stubbornly ambiguous either way. You can push for a read now, on partial evidence, or wait for something closer to certainty — and the clock is charging interest on that choice too.

read_aloud_variants.text: The logs finally give up the shape of it, and it's worse than a three-day story. The compromised VPN credential was used to log in, quietly, six weeks before the ransom note ever landed — six weeks of an account that should have raised an alert and never did, because nobody was watching that specific account closely enough to notice a login pattern that didn't belong to the person it was issued to. The exfiltration itself, a benefits-and-payroll export, happened three days before deployment; the door had been open for a month and a half before anyone walked through it with intent to encrypt. This incident did not begin at 06:10. It only became visible then.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The logs are still being read, line by line, against a claim that refuses to resolve cleanly.

narrative.present_tension: Certainty and speed are trading against each other, and the trade only gets worse the longer you hold both.
narrative.hidden_truth: The exfiltration is real: the attackers pulled a benefits-and-payroll export through the same compromised VPN link three days before the ransom note landed. The initial compromise itself is older still — six weeks of quiet access on that VPN account before the export, undetected the whole time.
sensory_details: log timestamps scrolling past faster than anyone can read them
a forensic analyst's coffee, also cold
narrative.emotional_undertone: The specific vertigo of a fact that exists but hasn't been proven yet.

nodes.title: The Silence Gets Expensive
nodes.summary: Waiting for certainty eats the clock. A local reporter is already asking why council tax payments have failed for a day and counting.
nodes.read_aloud: Waiting for certainty eats the clock in a way that doesn't show up on any dashboard. Council tax payments have now been down for the best part of a day, long enough that a local reporter starts asking why, in the tone of someone who already suspects the answer and is only confirming it for the record.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The silence is still costing you, in the specific currency of a reporter's patience running out.

narrative.present_tension: Every hour of waiting for forensic certainty is an hour someone outside the building spends filling the silence with their own theory.
sensory_details: a voicemail from the local paper, unreturned
the benefits helpline's hold music, looping
narrative.emotional_undertone: Diligence curdling into something that looks, from outside, like evasion.

nodes.title: Drafting the Line to Take
nodes.summary: Comms and legal draft the public statement, working from the same facts but disagreeing on how much certainty to commit to in writing before the picture is complete.
nodes.read_aloud: Comms and legal sit down to draft the line to take. Legal wants fewer numbers until the scope is confirmed — a figure published now and revised downward next week reads as either caution or incompetence, and there's no controlling which. Comms wants more detail now, on the theory that residents fill silence with worse guesses than the truth would give them. Both positions are reasonable. Neither drafter is wrong about the risk they're naming.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The draft statement sits on the table, still unresolved between two defensible instincts.

narrative.present_tension: Publishing early risks a number that has to be walked back; waiting risks residents hearing it from somewhere else first.
sensory_details: two marked-up drafts on the same table
a red pen moving faster over one than the other
narrative.emotional_undertone: A genuine disagreement between people trying to get the same thing right.

nodes.title: The Leader Wants This Managed
nodes.summary: The Leader and Chief Exec want a review before anything goes out — and they have three specific, defensible reasons, not just a preference for quiet.
nodes.read_aloud: The Leader and the Chief Exec want a look at the statement before it goes anywhere, and they're specific about why: the figures in the current draft are the forensics team's best estimate, not a confirmed count, and a number published today that has to be revised down next week reads as either panic or incompetence to residents who won't know which. There's also the ongoing report to Report Fraud to think about — publishing operational detail before the investigation has a shape could tip off exactly who they're trying to catch. And the draft, as written, implies a restoration date nobody on the technical side has actually confirmed. None of that is an instruction to hide anything. All of it is a reason to slow down, and slowing down has its own cost that the people making the case for caution are not the ones paying.

read_aloud_variants.text: The Leader and the Chief Exec want a look at the statement before it goes anywhere, and they're specific about why: unconfirmed figures, an ongoing report to Report Fraud, a restoration date nobody technical has actually signed off — and this time the Leader opens with a complaint of their own before any of that: half the senior team heard about the breach from their own staff's group chats before anyone official told them anything. The caution about the statement is the same caution as always. The fact that it's landing in a room that already doesn't trust the council's own information discipline is new.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The Leader's office again, the same three concerns on the table, still unresolved.

rumour_delivery.speaker: The Leader, directly, not through the special adviser this time
rumour_delivery.text: I'm not asking you to lie to anyone. I watched a neighbouring authority publish a casualty figure that turned out to be wrong, and I watched them spend eighteen months being 'the council that got the numbers wrong', not 'the council that got breached'. Tell me the number's solid and I'll sign off on it tonight.

rumour_delivery.speaker: The Leader's special adviser, standing just behind the Leader's chair
rumour_delivery.text: Nobody is asking you to lie. We're asking you to lead with what's confirmed and let the rest come out on its own timeline. That's not spin, that's sequencing.

rumour_delivery.speaker: The benefits service manager, on speakerphone from the emergency payments desk
rumour_delivery.text: I've got people who haven't been paid in three days asking me why the council won't say what's going on. I don't have a sequencing answer for them. I have a Thursday.

narrative.present_tension: Every voice in this room is naming a real risk. None of them is naming the same one, and the statement can't wait for all three to be satisfied.
sensory_details: a closed door with a raised-voice murmur behind it
a statement draft with more redactions than sentences
narrative.emotional_undertone: Competing, legitimate duties, with no version of tonight that fully honours all of them.

nodes.title: Going Public
nodes.summary: The statement goes out. Residents and press now know what you know; whether the regulator does too depends on what happened earlier in the incident.
nodes.read_aloud: The statement goes out. Residents, press and the regulator now know roughly what you know, at roughly the same time you know it, which is either the whole point of doing this properly or the last mistake you make, depending on who you ask by the end of the week. The ransom deadline does not pause to let the news cycle catch up.

read_aloud_variants.text: The statement goes out. Residents and press know what you know, at roughly the same time you know it — but the regulator isn't on that list, not yet, not from you. A public statement is not an ICO notification; the two feel similar from a podium and are not the same act, and nobody in the room has actually made the second one. That's still fixable — the seventy-two hours haven't closed, and the call can still be made, out of order as it will now look but not late — but it fixes itself for nobody. Somebody has to notice the gap and pick up the phone. The ransom deadline does not pause to let the news cycle, or the gap in your paperwork, catch up.

The short version goes out first, exactly as agreed: an incident, services affected, more detail as it's confirmed. It reads as thin to anyone wanting the whole picture tonight, and as responsible to anyone who remembers the neighbouring council that published a number it later had to walk back. The fuller account is still owed, not delivered — this bought time to get it right, not permission to skip it.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The statement is out there now, doing its slow work in inboxes and newsrooms, impossible to call back.

narrative.present_tension: Once it is public, every subsequent decision is made in front of an audience that was not in the room for the earlier ones.
sensory_details: a press office phone ringing before the statement has finished sending
a comment thread filling in real time
narrative.emotional_undertone: The relief of honesty, immediately followed by the weight of it.

nodes.title: The Policy's Fine Print
nodes.summary: The council's cyber insurer joins the call before the ransom decision, and their position is blunter than the policy document ever was.
nodes.read_aloud: The insurer's incident line delivers four facts, flatly, before anyone gets to a decision. Restoration and forensic costs are covered. A ransom payment to a group this plausibly linked to a sanctioned jurisdiction is not covered, and authorising one is very likely to void the policy outright. The recovery panel needs a verified backup status before it will sign off on a restore attempt. And central government already can't pay ransoms — the Home Office's own consultation response has committed to extending that ban to every public body in the country, councils included, though it isn't law yet. Only after all four facts land does the person on the line add anything of their own: it isn't really a coverage decision, they say. It's the decision.

read_aloud_variants.text: The insurer's incident line delivers four facts, flatly, before anyone gets to a decision. Restoration and forensic costs are covered. A ransom payment to a group this plausibly linked to a sanctioned jurisdiction is not covered, and authorising one is very likely to void the policy outright. The recovery panel needs a verified backup status before it will sign off on a restore attempt. And central government already can't pay ransoms — the Home Office's own consultation response has committed to extending that ban to every public body in the country, councils included, though it isn't law yet. Only after all four facts land does the person on the line add anything of their own: whoever's running this, they say, is moving fast — most of the attacker's own seventy-two hours are still sitting unused, which is not something they can say about every call like this one.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The insurer's position hasn't moved: covered for recovery, not covered for the ransom, and the two are not equally risky in ways that matter to them.

narrative.present_tension: The policy was written to make this decision look simple. Making it under a live deadline does not feel simple.
narrative.hidden_truth: The insurer already suspects the attacker is sanctions-adjacent from the ransom note's payment instructions alone; they are not guessing when they say the payment is unlikely to be covered.
sensory_details: a policy document, several tabs deep, open on a second screen
the insurer's loss adjuster taking notes audibly faster than the conversation warrants
narrative.emotional_undertone: The strange comfort of a decision that someone else has already priced.

nodes.title: The 72-Hour Clock, Kept
nodes.summary: The ICO notification goes out inside the statutory window — the one call on this list that is actually a legal duty, not a courtesy.
nodes.read_aloud: The call to the ICO goes out promptly, on evidence that is good enough rather than perfect. This is the one notification on your list that isn't optional: UK GDPR gives you seventy-two hours from becoming aware of a breach to tell the regulator, whether or not you're certain yet what happened, and this call goes out with most of that window still in hand. It comes back with a case reference — RFA1122334, written down twice to be sure — that will follow this incident through every report that mentions it from here on. Only the ICO reference number is checked against a clock.

read_aloud_variants.text: The call to the ICO goes out after the public statement, after residents and press already had the story, after someone in the room finally asked the question everyone had assumed someone else had already answered. It comes back with a case reference — RFA1122334 — attached to a notification submitted comfortably within the seventy-two hours: the statutory deadline is met, cleanly. Residents and the press heard first, which is an unusual sequence and one a post-incident review will want to understand — the law separates the duty to notify the regulator from any duty to inform the public, and doesn't dictate which comes first — but it does not, by itself, make this notification late or non-compliant.

The call to the ICO goes out, and for once you're not reading from a script of caveats: the logs are read, the exfiltration is confirmed, and the notification carries a fact instead of a hedge. It comes back with a case reference — RFA1122334 — attached to a notification that took its time and arrived complete rather than early and partial. UK GDPR's seventy-two hours are comfortably unspent; the only real question, and it's a fair one for a post-incident review to ask, is whether the wait for certainty was itself the right call when the ICO's own guidance would have accepted a report on partial evidence, updated later. Only the ICO reference is ever checked against a clock, and this one arrives with time comfortably to spare.

The call to the ICO goes out, on evidence that is good enough rather than perfect, after a wait for certainty that took longer than the decision strictly needed. It comes back with a case reference — RFA1122334 — and a timestamp that sits comfortably inside the seventy-two hours UK GDPR actually allows. The statutory duty is met without qualification; the more interesting question, for anyone reviewing this afterwards, is whether reporting sooner on partial evidence — which the ICO's own guidance explicitly welcomes — would have served everyone better than waiting for a fact that turned out to cost hours nobody got back. Only the ICO reference is ever checked against a clock, and the clock still has room in it.

The call to the ICO goes out promptly, and this time 'on the evidence you have' means something more than a hedge: the logs are read and the exfiltration is confirmed before you ever pick up the phone. It comes back with a case reference — RFA1122334 — attached to a notification that is both timely and complete, which the caller on the other end notes is rarer than you'd think. This is the one notification on your list that isn't optional — UK GDPR gives you seventy-two hours from becoming aware of a breach to tell the regulator — and this call goes out early, with a definite answer instead of a probable one. Only the ICO reference is checked against a clock.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The ICO notification sits logged and timestamped, a fact nobody can later dispute.

narrative.present_tension: A prompt, imperfect notification will always read better in hindsight than a slower, complete one — and only the ICO clock actually enforces that lesson, since the law itself is content with either as long as both land inside it.
sensory_details: a call reference number, written down twice to be sure
the strange calm of a deadline actually met
narrative.emotional_undertone: A small, procedural act of courage that nobody in the room will call brave.

nodes.title: Overrun
nodes.summary: The delay costs everything containable. Encryption reaches backups that weren't properly segmented.
nodes.read_aloud: The delay costs everything that was still containable. Encryption reaches the backups — the ones that were supposed to be segmented, and weren't, quite, in the way the architecture diagram had always promised. There is no clean floor left to stand on.

read_aloud_variants.text: The restore job stalls on the same backups the architecture diagram swore were segmented from the rest of the network. They weren't, quite. You already told residents and the regulator that refusing to pay was the responsible choice. It still might have been — but there is no clean floor left to stand on, and the statement is already out there, unable to be unsaid.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The overrun sits where you left it: backups gone, and no version of events where that isn't the headline.

narrative.present_tension: The last safety net you were counting on was never as separate as the diagram said it was.
sensory_details: a backup job log ending mid-line
a silence where a recovery plan used to be
narrative.emotional_undertone: A plan failing exactly where you were told it couldn't.

nodes.title: It Gets Out Anyway
nodes.summary: A journalist files an FOI request that the softened statement can't survive. The story is no longer the breach — it's the silence.
nodes.read_aloud: A journalist files an FOI request the softened statement was never built to survive, and the story turns overnight. It is no longer the breach. It is the silence around the breach, and silence makes for a much better headline than an honest technical failure ever would.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The story has already turned, and there is no version of the softened statement that survives a second reading.

narrative.present_tension: A cover-up only has to fail once to become the entire story.
sensory_details: an FOI response with a due date already passed
a phone that has started ringing from numbers you don't recognise
narrative.emotional_undertone: The specific shame of being caught managing the truth rather than telling it.

nodes.title: ENDING: The Playbook Worked
nodes.summary: Regulator notified in time (case RFA1122334), ransom refused, backups held. Services return within the week — the decision window closes clean, though the full recovery bill and the ICO's finding on the stale VPN credential still have months left to run.
nodes.read_aloud: Regulator notified in time, ransom refused, backups held. Services return within the week, in the right order, without a second crisis hiding behind the first one. It does not end there: the recovery bill runs into the millions before the last system is rebuilt from clean images, and it is the better part of a year before every service is fully itself again — a fast, well-run response doesn't shrink the actual cost of a breach this size, it just keeps it from becoming a second, worse story. When the ICO closes its file against case RFA1122334, months later, it isn't with 'no further action' — it's a reprimand, not a fine, for the stale VPN credential that let this happen in the first place. The response bought you that: a regulator willing to say, in writing, that what you did afterwards was right, even though what let the attacker in wasn't. The incident becomes the case study you cite, calmly, the next time someone in a budget meeting asks whether any of this was worth paying for — not because it ended cheaply, but because it ended honestly.

read_aloud_variants.text: Regulator notified in time, ransom refused, backups held. Services return within the week, in the right order, without a second crisis hiding behind the first one. It does not end there — and this time the ICO's reprimand carries two findings, not one: the stale VPN credential that let this happen, and a general press statement that stood in for direct notification to the specific residents whose benefits and payroll data was actually taken, when the risk to them individually should have triggered a direct letter or call. The recovery bill still runs into the millions; the better part of a year still passes before every service is fully itself again. The response was fast and well-run in every way that touched the council's own systems. It was not, in the end, quite complete in the one way that touched the residents whose data left the building — the reprimand is careful to say so.

Regulator notified in time, ransom refused, backups held — but every deliberation that got you to this decision cost real hours of council tax, benefits and case-management systems staying down while the picture was confirmed. Services take closer to a fortnight to return, not a week, because a good decision made slowly still costs the time it took to make it slowly. It does not end there: the recovery bill runs into the millions, and it is the better part of a year before every service is fully itself again. When the ICO closes its file, it isn't with 'no further action' — a reprimand, not a fine, for the stale VPN credential that let this happen. The response was right. Right and unhurried are not, in an incident like this one, quite the same thing.

narrative.present_tension: Resolved, at the cost of every easy shortcut you didn't take — and at a cost that was never going to be small, whichever choices were made.
sensory_details: a services dashboard turning green, department by department
a debrief slide with no red text on it
narrative.emotional_undertone: The quiet, almost anticlimactic satisfaction of a plan that simply worked.

decisions_that_mattered: Isolation was treated as a settled organisational capability, not a gamble — the network came down cleanly because the plan for that already existed before tonight.
The recovery decision waited for the backup status report and the affected-data schedule rather than a clock, so the 'clean' restore was actually clean.
Regulator notification happened inside the 72-hour window on the facts as they were understood at the time, not on a guess.
organisational_actions: Audit credential rotation on remote-access and VPN accounts — the entry point here was a stale credential, and the response being excellent didn't make that root cause go away.
Run an isolation drill that doesn't assume the isolation plan works; this response only stayed calm because that assumption happened to hold.
Schedule a clean-image restore test outside of a live incident, so 'the backups are good' is verified, not asserted, next time.
Confirm out-of-band communication channels are actually tested and known to staff before they're needed at 2am.

nodes.title: ENDING: Bought, Not Solved
nodes.summary: The payment buys a decryption key and a quieter fortnight. It also buys an NCA enquiry into sanctioned-entity payment risk, a voided insurance policy, and an internal audit finding that follows you for years — filed just as the sector-wide payment ban the government had already committed to finally clears Parliament.
nodes.read_aloud: The payment buys a working decryption key and a quieter fortnight than the alternative would have given you. It also buys an NCA enquiry into sanctioned-entity payment risk, a cyber insurance policy the insurer voids exactly as promised, and an internal audit finding with your name near the top of it that will still be circulating in meetings years from now, cited by people who weren't there. The insurer's loss adjuster, it turns out, took very good notes. Eighteen months later, when the promised ban on public-sector ransom payments finally becomes law, your finding is the case study the second-reading debate keeps coming back to.

narrative.present_tension: Deferred, at a rate of interest nobody quoted you up front — the insurer quoted it, and you heard it, and paid anyway.
sensory_details: a decryption key that actually works, disappointingly ordinary to look at
a claim rejection letter, impressively fast for an insurer
an audit finding, filed and unforgettable
narrative.emotional_undertone: Relief with a second bill still to arrive.

decisions_that_mattered: Payment was chosen as the fast route back to service, against NCSC guidance that a working decryptor is never guaranteed and against the policy wording nobody had reread since renewal.
The insurer's notification terms were breached by the timing or manner of the payment decision, not by the fact of paying itself.
Law enforcement and reporting obligations were treated as secondary to restoring service, which is exactly the ordering the eventual enquiry focused on.
organisational_actions: Read the current cyber insurance policy wording before the next incident, specifically the notification-timing and payment-authorisation clauses — not during one.
Confirm the current reporting contacts (Report Fraud at reportfraud.police.uk, NCSC Incident Management) are known and reachable before an incident, not discovered mid-crisis.
Review organisational readiness for the public-sector ransomware payment ban that is already committed to and moving through Parliament — payment may not be a lawful option much longer.
Establish who has authority to approve a ransom payment in principle, in advance, rather than deciding it under deadline pressure.

nodes.title: ENDING: Overrun
nodes.summary: Benefits payments fail for weeks. National coverage. The ICO opens a full investigation; the Leader resigns before it concludes.
nodes.read_aloud: Benefits payments fail for weeks, not days. National coverage follows, then a full ICO investigation, and the Leader resigns before it concludes — not because anyone forced the point, but because there is no longer a version of staying that doesn't look worse than going.

read_aloud_variants.text: The payment went out, and the decryptor that came back doesn't work — not fully, not on the systems that matter. NCSC's guidance said as much, in the small print nobody read aloud in the room: paying buys no guarantee. Benefits payments fail for weeks, not days. National coverage follows, then a full ICO investigation that now has a second question to ask — not just how you were breached, but why the council paid a group under sanctions scrutiny and still ended up here. The Leader resigns before it concludes.

narrative.present_tension: Ended, in the worst way an incident can end: everyone still has questions, and nobody in the room can answer them any more.
sensory_details: a national news chyron with the council's name in it
an empty chair where the Leader used to sit
narrative.emotional_undertone: An incident that outlasted everyone's authority to fix it.

decisions_that_mattered: Containment and investigation fell behind the attacker's own timeline, and nobody re-scoped the response once it became clear the original plan wasn't going to hold.
Political and reputational pressure was allowed to compete with the technical response for the same attention, rather than being delegated to run in parallel with it.
Escalation past Gold Command's own authority — political, legal, financial — didn't happen early enough for anyone above that level to help before it became unrecoverable.
organisational_actions: Clarify Gold Command's actual authority boundaries and escalation triggers before an incident, so 'this needs escalating' is a known threshold, not a judgement call made too late.
Run a resident-notification exercise at scale — this ending is defined by services failing publicly for weeks, which is also a communications capacity problem, not only a technical one.
Test isolation and segmentation capability under realistic load, not just in principle; slow containment is what let this run past the point of recovery.
Build a standing plan for sustained multi-week incident command, since the assumption that Gold Command is a short, intense burst is itself a single point of failure.

nodes.title: ENDING: The Cover-Up Was Worse
nodes.summary: The breach was survivable. The softened statement was not. You are named, personally, in the resulting report.
nodes.read_aloud: The breach, on its own, was survivable — councils survive breaches every year. The softened statement was not. The resulting report names you, personally, not for the ransomware, but for the sentence you approved that everyone in the room knew wasn't quite true when you approved it.

narrative.present_tension: Ended, with your own signature the last piece of evidence anyone needed.
sensory_details: a report with your name in a sentence you can't unread
a phone that has stopped ringing, which is somehow worse
narrative.emotional_undertone: The specific loneliness of being right about the technology and wrong about the truth.

decisions_that_mattered: A public statement was approved that was softer than the facts on the ground supported, trading a harder truth now for an easier one that didn't hold.
The choice to lead with a holding statement, or not, and who signed off on its wording, became the thing the eventual report actually turned on — not the ransomware itself.
Staff and public communications diverged, and that gap was the evidence that made the statement look like a cover-up even if it wasn't originally intended as one.
organisational_actions: Establish a public-statement approval chain that includes someone whose job is specifically to check statements against the facts as currently known, separate from the person under pressure to reassure.
Practise holding-statement-first discipline: a short, true, incomplete statement now beats a fuller one that turns out to be wrong.
Review how staff communications and public communications are kept consistent, so neither one is the version that gets contradicted later.
Treat 'this line isn't quite true yet' as a stop-the-line moment for sign-off, not a wording problem to smooth over.

nodes.title: ENDING: The Clock Outlasted You
nodes.summary: Another sleepless night at the incident desk finally costs more than the investigation gained. Someone else has to finish reading the logs.
nodes.read_aloud: You told yourself one more pass through the logs, one more sleepless stretch, and the incident would finally hold still. It doesn't. Somewhere in the small hours, the room tips sideways and does not come back. Somebody else finishes reading the logs. The incident does not pause to wait for you to recover, and neither, it turns out, does anyone's memory of who was meant to be in charge.

narrative.present_tension: Ended, by the one variable nobody put on the incident dashboard: you.
sensory_details: a keyboard, still warm, under someone else's hands now
a shift handover written by a colleague, careful not to say too much
narrative.emotional_undertone: The cost of treating your own limits as the one risk not worth logging.

decisions_that_mattered: Personal stamina was spent as if it were an unlimited resource, on the assumption that the incident needed one continuous person at the centre of it more than it needed a rested one.
No handover point was built into the response, so there was no moment where stepping back was a planned decision rather than a collapse.
The people around Gold Command didn't have a trigger for stepping in — the incident had escalation paths for everything except its own commander.
organisational_actions: Build shift rotation into the incident command structure from the start of any multi-day response, not as an afterthought once someone is already exhausted.
Log personal capacity and welfare as a tracked incident risk, with an explicit threshold for when someone is required to step back, not just permitted to.
Establish a deputy or shadow for Gold Command whose job includes watching the commander, not only the incident.
Write handover protocols in advance so a change of commander mid-incident is a smooth, planned transfer of context rather than an emergency improvisation.

routes.label: Isolate the network immediately
routes.stakes: Cut now, on instinct: isolation lands, deterministically — this is a decision, not a dice roll — but cutting blind before scope is known means you can't yet say what, if anything, got out before the switch was thrown.
routes.hook: Every minute of hesitation is spreading. The switch, at least, is not in question.

narrative.player_intent: Act immediately on incomplete information.
narrative.dramatic_role: Deliberately not a skill test: whether isolation works is a matter of organisational capability (segmentation, tested procedures, staff availability) established before the incident, not a coin flip made by Gold Command in the room. The genuine uncertainty here is what already left before you cut the cord, which is why isolating blind still costs a point of exposure — not because the isolation itself might fail.

routes.label: Verify scope before cutting services
routes.stakes: Caution costs blast radius and six hours off the clock; verification takes time the encryption doesn't.
routes.hook: Cutting services blind has its own cost. So does not cutting them.

narrative.player_intent: Confirm the picture before acting.
narrative.dramatic_role: The cautious road, guaranteed to cost exposure.

routes.label: Stand up formal Gold/Silver/Bronze first
routes.stakes: Twenty minutes of structure now, paid back in coordination later.
routes.hook: It looks like delay while the fires are still small.

narrative.player_intent: Build command structure before acting.
narrative.dramatic_role: The investment road.

routes.label: Stand up Gold Command properly and work the response as parallel workstreams
routes.stakes: Staff need telling, law enforcement needs a decision, and the investigation needs to start — none of them has to wait for the other two to finish first.
routes.hook: The helpdesk phone hasn't stopped ringing since the network dropped, and it isn't the only thing that needs attention at once.

narrative.player_intent: Begin the response as Gold Command, not as a checklist.
narrative.dramatic_role: The disciplined entry into gold_command_hub, where staff comms and law-enforcement engagement can be addressed in either order before the investigation proper begins.

routes.label: Address staff communications
routes.stakes: Nothing about this workstream depends on the others being finished first — it only depends on nobody having addressed it yet.
routes.hook: The helpdesk queue doesn't check whether law enforcement's been called before it keeps growing.

narrative.player_intent: Turn attention to staff communications.
narrative.dramatic_role: Hub spoke; disappears from the menu once staff_informed_safely or staff_comms_leaked is set, either way.

routes.label: Address law enforcement and NCSC reporting
routes.stakes: This workstream doesn't wait on the others either. It's a phone call, not a dependency.
routes.hook: The report form is still open in a tab from an hour ago.

narrative.player_intent: Turn attention to the reporting decision.
narrative.dramatic_role: Hub spoke; disappears from the menu once law_enforcement_call_addressed is set.

routes.label: Move to the technical investigation
routes.stakes: The investigation doesn't have to wait for the other two workstreams to close out — though whichever of them you've already resolved will shape how it goes.
routes.hook: Forensics has been ready to start since the logs first came in. Nobody's stopping you.

narrative.player_intent: Begin the technical investigation, whatever else is or isn't resolved yet.
narrative.dramatic_role: The graduation spoke out of the hub's front-loaded triage and into the main investigative branch; always available, since m040/m041b already read law_enforcement_notified live and don't need this route to gate on it.

routes.label: Send an all-staff email now, on the network you're not sure is clean
routes.stakes: Fast, and free, and exactly the channel you can't currently vouch for — if a mailbox is still readable by someone who isn't you, or a screenshot makes it further than the building, that's on this email.
routes.hook: The draft is one paragraph long. Sending it is the only slow part.

narrative.player_intent: Warn staff quickly, accepting the channel risk.
narrative.dramatic_role: The fast, unverified road; a real chance of the message becoming the leak it was meant to prevent.

routes.label: Stand up the out-of-band phone and text tree instead
routes.stakes: Slower, and it costs a call to HR to find out whether the emergency contact list is even current — but nothing about it depends on a system you don't trust yet.
routes.hook: Half the numbers on the list are two jobs out of date. It still beats silence.

narrative.player_intent: Warn staff safely, at the cost of time and coordination.
narrative.dramatic_role: The slower, verifiably safe road — the one real incident responders actually reach for when the compromised channel is the usual one.

routes.label: Say nothing formally yet; let it filter through operationally
routes.stakes: Nobody has to defend a message that was never sent — but the helpdesk queue and the group chats will write their own version of events faster than you're writing yours.
routes.hook: By the time you have a line to take, three hundred people already have their own.

narrative.player_intent: Delay any formal internal communication.
narrative.dramatic_role: The road into a staff-side leak, symmetrical with the press-side cover-up branch elsewhere in the graph.

routes.label: Report to Report Fraud and request NCSC incident management support now, before forensics goes any deeper
routes.stakes: An outside agency in the room this early, in exchange for a formal, defensible evidence chain from here on.
routes.hook: The report takes four minutes to submit. It costs you nothing but the interruption.

narrative.player_intent: Engage police reporting and NCSC support before the forensic work proceeds.
narrative.dramatic_role: The procedurally sound but socially costly choice; strengthens the forensics test whenever it's eventually run.

routes.label: Handle it internally for now, without looping in law enforcement
routes.stakes: Working without an audience, at the cost of a weaker evidence trail if this ever needs proving to someone else — and this decision doesn't come back around once made.
routes.hook: Nobody has actually decided not to report it. It just isn't happening right now.

narrative.player_intent: Delay law enforcement engagement.
narrative.dramatic_role: The quieter road; weakens whatever forensics test is eventually run and nudges exposure regardless of outcome. One-way — law_enforcement_call_addressed removes this spoke from the hub once chosen, either way.

routes.label: Push to reopen benefits payments before forensics finish
routes.stakes: Residents need their payments; forensics needs the network exactly as it is — and forcing the issue against professional advice this early spends most of the political capital you'll want when the Leader pushes back an hour from now.
routes.hook: The benefits helpline has stopped ringing. That is not the same as stopped needing.

narrative.player_intent: Prioritise service restoration over investigation.
narrative.dramatic_role: Early route into the political pressure encounter.

routes.label: Isolate now, later than you should have
routes.stakes: Contained, finally — but the record will show you waited.
routes.hook: The switch is still there. It always was.

narrative.player_intent: Contain the incident, belatedly.
narrative.dramatic_role: The delayed correction; marks late_containment permanently.

routes.label: Try to buy time before isolating
routes.stakes: Every extra minute of uncontained spread is a minute you can't get back, and this one costs half a day of runway.
routes.hook: The backups are supposed to be segmented from this. Supposed to be.

narrative.player_intent: Delay containment further.
narrative.dramatic_role: The road to the overrun ending.

routes.label: Isolate the network now
routes.stakes: Structure is in place; now the network comes down.
routes.hook: Gold, Silver and Bronze all agree: cut it.

narrative.player_intent: Contain with formal structure already standing.
narrative.dramatic_role: The disciplined containment route.

routes.label: Verify scope first
routes.stakes: Even with structure standing, verification still costs blast radius and the same six hours it always does.
routes.hook: Command structure doesn't slow the encryption down. Only isolation does that.

narrative.player_intent: Verify before acting, with structure in place.
narrative.dramatic_role: The cautious route out of the structured branch.

routes.label: Work the logs for a definitive answer
routes.stakes: A clean read of the logs, worked alone, turns a suspicion into a fact you can put in a regulator notification — if you can read it right without help, in the four hours it takes.
routes.hook: The timestamps are all there. Reading them right, under this clock, is the hard part.

narrative.player_intent: Establish whether data actually left the network, without outside support.
narrative.dramatic_role: The core investigative test, run at the harder difficulty available when law enforcement wasn't looped in.

routes.label: Work the logs for a definitive answer, with NCSC's incident management support
routes.stakes: A clean read of the logs turns a suspicion into a fact you can put in a regulator notification — and this time you're not reading them alone.
routes.hook: Priya C., NCSC's incident management analyst working the case since the report went in, has seen this exact log pattern before. That's worth more than another pot of coffee.

narrative.player_intent: Establish whether data actually left the network, with formal backup.
narrative.dramatic_role: The reward for the reporting decision: a meaningfully better test at the adventure's core investigation.

routes.label: Notify the ICO now, on the evidence you have
routes.stakes: Notify inside the statutory window on the evidence you have, whatever state it's in — this is the one call the clock actually measures.
routes.hook: A timely notification reads better in every report that follows, whether the evidence behind it is definite or still partial.

narrative.player_intent: Notify the ICO immediately, inside the legal deadline.
narrative.dramatic_role: The fast, defensible compliance route; available whether or not exfiltration is confirmed yet — regulator_call's read_aloud_variants pick the framing (definite vs. partial evidence) to match the actual state.

routes.label: Wait for forensic certainty before notifying
routes.stakes: Certainty is worth having. It is not free — a full day spent, and a reporter's questions arriving before you have an answer for them.
routes.hook: Somewhere outside this room, a reporter is already noticing the silence.
